Chunk # 0.
Organelle isolation: functional mitochondria from
mouse liver, muscle and cultured ﬁlroblasts
Christian Frezza, Sara Cipolat & Luca Scorrano
Dulbecco-Telethon Institute, Venetian Institute of Molecular Medicine, Via Orus 2, 35129 Padova, Italy Correspondence should be addressed to
L.S (luca.scorrano@unipd.it) Published online 22 February 2007; doi:10.1038/nprot.2006.478
Mitochondria participate in key metabolic reactions of the cell and regulate crucial signaling pathways including apoptosis Although
several approaches are available to study mitochondrial function in situ are available, investigating functional mitochondria that have
been isolated from different tissues and from cultured cells offers still more unmatched advantages This protocol illustrates a
step-by-step procedure to obtain functional mitochondria with high yield from cells grown in culture, liver and muscle The isolation
procedures described here require 1–2 hours, depending on the source of the organelles The polarographic analysis can be completed
in 1 hour INTRODUCTION
Mitochondria are central organelles controlling the life and death of
the cell They participate in key metabolic reactions, synthesize
most of the ATP and regulate a number of signaling cascades,
including apoptosis1 Since the early years of ‘‘hard-core’’ bioenergetics when mechan-
isms behind energy conservation were avidly investigated, mito-
chondrial research has beneﬁted from the availability of
preparations of organelles isolated from tissues We owe this to
the pioneering work of George Palade and coworkers, who in the
late 1940s developed a protocol to isolate mitochondria, based on
differential centrifugation2 They built on the earlier work of
Bensley and Hoerr3, who isolated a mixed membranous fraction
by centrifugation from freeze-thawed guinea-pig liver that was
probably enriched in mitochondria The intuition of Palade was to
apply differential centrifugation to allow for separation of the
constituents of the cell based on their different sedimentation
properties following mechanical homogenization of the tissue This approach was a real Copernican revolution for mitochondrial
research, allowing the isolation of pure organelles with high yields As a practical consequence, in the subsequent 20 years, we saw such
amazing discoveries: the mechanism of energy conservation4;t h e
identiﬁcation of mitochondrial DNA5,6a n do fi m p o r to fm i t o -
chondrial precursor proteins7; the deﬁnition of mitochondrial
ultrastructure, with the development of the so-called ‘‘Palade’s
model’’8; and last but not least, the discovery of inner mitochon-
drial membrane channels9 After almost 15 years during which mitochondria left the center
stage of biomedical research, they made their grand reentre ´ein the
1990s, following the discovery that they amplify apoptosis by
releasing cytochrome cand other intermembrane space proteins
required to activate fully effector caspases10,11.A l t h o u g hi ta p p e a r s
clear that mitochondria play a crucial role in apoptosis, the precise
mechanism by which cytochrome cis released remains a matter of
intense debate and research12 Moreover, evidence is mounting on
the role of this organelle in several pathophysiological processes,
including neurodegeneration13, neuronal morphogenesis and plas-
ticity14and infertility15 These ﬁndings, added to the results of old
and new areas of research, aimed at unraveling the basic biological
mechanisms of mitochondrial function From the transport ofmetabolites and ions, to the elucidation of the mechanisms and
proteins involved in protein import, and to the dynamic behavior
of mitochondria, all of these ﬁelds beneﬁt greatly from the
availability of isolated, pure organelles This protocol describes how to obtain functional, puriﬁed, intact
mitochondria from three different sources: liver16, skeletal muscle17
and cultured cells18 These variants intend to be exempliﬁcative and
not exhaustive, as they do not cover the different sources from
which mitochondria can be isolated For example, isolation of
mitochondria from yeast cells is tailored on the mechanical and
osmotic characteristics of these lower eucaryotes19 Since our
intention is to give a general framework for different organs and
for cultured cells that can be in any case modiﬁed by the individual
researcher, following exactly these protocols is best suited only for
isolation of organelles from the described tissues and cells How-
ever, our experience indicates that the protocol used with ﬁbro-
blasts can be adopted without modiﬁcation to isolate mitochondria
from other cell lines such as HeLa and the prostate cancer cell line
LnCaP  On the other hand, the protocols to isolate mitochondria
from organs other than muscle and liver differ from the ones
described here We therefore strongly advise the reader to refer to
published protocols speciﬁc for brain20, brown adipose tissue21,
and heart22 It should be stressed that protocols available to isolate mitochon-
dria are somewhat differ from ours, especially in the speeds of the
differential centrifugation steps and in the sugar used as osmolyte in
the isolation buffer While in our experience small changes in the
sedimentation speeds (600 vs 800 g, 7,000 vs
summary:
SPR:
- This protocol describes a step-by-step procedure to isolate functional mitochondria from mouse liver, muscle, and cultured fibroblasts.
- Mitochondria are essential organelles involved in key metabolic reactions and the regulation of signaling pathways, including apoptosis.
- The isolation procedures outlined here require 1-2 hours, depending on the source of the organelles.
- The protocol provides variations for isolating mitochondria from different tissues and cultured cells.
- It is recommended to refer to specific protocols for isolating mitochondria from brain, brown adipose tissue, and heart.

reported that the use of monosaccharides such as mannitol results in
better coupled isolated mitochondria23,24 In our experience the use
of mannitol did not improve the quality of our mitochondrial
preparations Should the reader ﬁnd that quality or yield of
mitochondria isolated using our protocol is unsatisfactory, it is
advisable to try to substitute sucrose with a monosaccharide
like mannitol The ultimate goal of a mitochondrial isolation is to
obtain organelles as pure and as functional as possible We strongly
advise, especially if mitochondria are used in functional assays (e.g.,

coupling of the preparation using an oxygen electrode These
protocols therefore end with a description of how to measure
mitochondrial respiration to ascertain the quality of the preparation Well-coupled mitochondria are the ﬁrst step to achieving reliable,
reproducible results in assays aimed at investigating the mechanisms
of mitochondrial involvement in complex biological phenomena.In conclusion, these protocols represent a valuable starting point
to obtain pure mitochondria from tissues and cells Isolated
mitochondria can then be used to study the function of the

summary:
- Dissolve 372.2 g of EDTA in 500 ml of distilled water, adjust pH to 7.4 using Tris powder, bring the solution to 1 liter and store at 4°C.
- Dissolve 10 g of BSA in 100 ml of distilled water and store at -20°C.
- Dissolve 136.1 g of KH2PO4 in 500 ml of distilled water, adjust pH to 7.4 using Tris powder, bring the solution to 1 liter and store at 4°C.
- Dissolve 4.7 mg of ADP in 1 ml of distilled water, adjust pH to 7.4, prepare 100 ml aliquots and store in the dark at -20°C for up to 6 months.
- Dissolve 5.1 mg of FCCP in 1 ml of absolute ethanol, store at -20°C. Dilute the stock solution to 100 mM by adding 10 ml of 20 mM FCCP in 2 ml of absolute ethanol, just prior to use.
- Glass/Teﬂon Potter Elvehjem homogenizers: The left one (5 ml) is suitable for isolating mitochondria from cells, while the right one (30 ml) is more appropriate for isolation from tissues.
- Dissolve 9.2 g of glutamic acid and 4.2 g of malic acid in 100 ml of distilled water, adjust pH to 7.4 with Tris base, add water to bring the volume to 250 ml, prepare 10 ml aliquots and store at -20°C for up to 6 months.
- Dissolve 3.0 g of succinic acid in 30 ml of distilled water, adjust pH with Tris base, add water to make up the volume to 50 ml, prepare 10 ml aliquots and store at -20°C for up to 6 months.
- Dissolve 4.7 mg of rotenone in 6 ml of absolute ethanol, mix well for complete dissolution. Protect the stock solution from direct light using aluminum foil.
- Dissolve 5.2 g of ascorbic acid in 50 ml of distilled water, adjust pH to 7.4 and store at -20°C for up to 6 months.
- Dissolve 0.36 g of TMPD in 50 ml of distilled water, adjust pH to 7.4, store at -20°C for up to 6 months.
- Dissolve 50 mg of antimycin A in 2 ml of absolute ethanol. Dilute the stock solution to 25 mg/ml by adding 2 ml of 25 mg/ml antimycin A in 2 ml of absolute ethanol, just prior to use.
- Buffer for cell and mouse liver mitochondria isolation (IB c): Prepare 100 ml of IB by adding 10 ml of 0.1 M Tris-MOPS, 1 ml of EGTA/Tris, and 20 ml of 1 M sucrose. Adjust pH to 7.4.
- Buffer 1 for muscle mitochondria isolation (IB m1): Prepare 100 ml of IB m1 by mixing 6.7 ml of 1 M sucrose, 5 ml of 1 M Tris/HCl, 5 ml of 1 M KCl, 1 ml of 1 M EDTA, and 2 ml of 10% BSA. Adjust pH to 7.4.
- Buffer 2 for muscle mitochondria isolation (IB m2): Prepare 100 ml of IB m2 by mixing 25 ml of 1 M sucrose, 3 ml of 0.1 M EGTA/Tris, and 1 ml of 1 M Tris/HCl. Adjust pH to 7.4.

topics.justification: Dissolve 5.1 mg of FCCP in 1 ml of absolute ethanol, store at -20°C. Dilute the stock solution to 100 mM by adding 10 ml of 20 mM FCCP in 2 ml of absolute ethanol, just prior to use.

topics.topic: Selection of homogenizers for mitochondria isolation
topics.justification: Glass/Teﬂon Potter Elvehjem homogenizers: The left one (5 ml) is suitable for isolating mitochondria from cells, while the right one (30 ml) is more appropriate for isolation from tissues.

topics.topic: Preparation of glutamic acid and malic acid solution
topics.justification: Dissolve 9.2 g of glutamic acid and 4.2 g of malic acid in 100 ml of distilled water, adjust pH to 7.4 with Tris base, add water to bring the volume to 250 ml, prepare 10 ml aliquots and store at -20°C for up to 6 months.

topics.topic: Preparation of succinic acid solution
topics.justification: Dissolve 3.0 g of succinic acid in 30 ml of distilled water, adjust pH with Tris base, add water to make up the volume to 50 ml, prepare 10 ml aliquots and store at -20°C for up to 6 months.

topics.topic: Preparation of rotenone stock solution
topics.justification: Dissolve 4.7 mg of rotenone in 6 ml of absolute ethanol, mix well for complete dissolution. Protect the stock solution from direct light using aluminum foil.

topics.topic: Preparation of ascorbic acid solution
topics.justification: Dissolve 5.2 g of ascorbic acid in 50 ml of distilled water, adjust pH to 7.4 and store at -20°C for up to 6 months.

topics.topic: Preparation of TMPD solution
topics.justification: Dissolve 0.36 g of TMPD in 50 ml of distilled water, adjust pH to 7.4, store at -20°C for up to 6 months.

topics.topic: Preparation of antimycin A stock solution
topics.justification: Dissolve 50 mg of antimycin A in 2 ml of absolute ethanol. Dilute the stock solution to 25 mg/ml by adding 2 ml of 25 mg/ml antimycin A in 2 ml of absolute ethanol, just prior to use.

topics.topic: Preparation of buffer for cell and mouse liver mitochondria isolation (IB c)
topics.justification: Prepare 100 ml of IB by adding 10 ml of 0.1 M Tris-MOPS, 1 ml of EGTA/Tris, and 20 ml of 1 M sucrose. Adjust pH to 7.4.

topics.topic: Preparation of buffer 1 for muscle mitochondria isolation (IB m1)
topics.justification: Prepare 100 ml of IB m1 by mixing 6.7 ml of 1 M sucrose, 5 ml of 1 M Tris/HCl, 5 ml of 1 M KCl, 1 ml of 1 M EDTA, and 2 ml of 10% BSA. Adjust pH to 7.4.

topics.topic: Preparation of buffer 2 for muscle mitochondria isolation (IB m2)
topics.justification: Prepare 100 ml of IB m2 by mixing 25 ml of 1 M sucrose, 3 ml of 0.1 M EGTA/Tris, and 1 ml of 1 M Tris/HCl. Adjust pH to 7.4.

summary: Summary 5: This protocol outlines the isolation of mitochondria from mouse embryonic fibroblasts (MEFs) and mouse liver. To avoid Ca2+ contamination, glassware should be washed with bidistilled water. Buffers should be prepared on the same day to prevent bacterial/yeast growth. The pH of solutions should be measured at 25°C. MEFs should be washed with PBS and detached using a cell scraper. Centrifugation at 600 g for 10 min at 4°C is necessary. Homogenization should be performed with a Teﬂon pestle at 1,600 r.p.m. Precooling glassware in an ice-bath is crucial to minimize phospholipase and protease activation. Centrifugation at 600 g for 10 min at 4°C is required. The pellet should be washed with ice-cold IB and resuspended. Centrifugation at 7,000 g for 10 min at 4°C is necessary. The supernatant should be discarded and the pellet resuspended. Mitochondria concentration can be measured using the Biuret method. The mitochondrial suspension should be stored on ice and used within 1-3 hours. Proper handling and storage are essential for maintaining functionality. This protocol can also be applied to isolating mitochondria from the liver.

summary:
Revised Summary:

This article presents a comprehensive protocol for isolating mitochondria from mouse liver and skeletal muscle. The protocol involves removing the liver/muscle, washing, mincing, and homogenization with a glass-Teflon potter. Centrifugation at specific speeds and durations is then performed to separate the components based on density and isolate the mitochondria. Collagen digestion with trypsin is carried out to break down collagen and facilitate the resuspension of the mitochondria. The functional responses of the mitochondria are measured using the Biuret method. The article includes figures illustrating the timing of isolation from mouse liver and skeletal muscle. It is crucial to store the mitochondria without dilution to maintain their functionality, and they should be used within 1-3 hours for optimal results.

summary: Summary 4: The respiratory chain complexes I, III, and IV can be inhibited by glutamate (5 mM) or malate (2.5 mM), while complexes II, III, and IV can be inhibited by succinate (5 mM) and rotenone (2 mM). Complex IV can be activated by ascorbate (6 mM) and TMPD (300 mM), but inhibited by antimycin A (0.25 mg/ml). To study specific complexes, add the appropriate concentrations of substrates and inhibitors. The oxygen consumption rate should be higher than with buffer alone, indicating functional mitochondria. Adding ADP (100-150 mM) will increase oxygen consumption, known as "state 3" respiration. The rate of oxygen consumption should be higher than with substrates alone, indicating well-coupled mitochondria. After ADP exhaustion, oxygen consumption will slow down, referred to as "state 4" respiration. Adding the uncoupler FCCP (60-100 nM) will increase respiration temporarily. Check the troubleshooting table for potential issues and solutions. Measure oxygen consumption for 5 minutes and then stop recording. Glutamate, malate, succinate, rotenone, antimycin A, and FCCP are key entities involved in modulating mitochondrial respiration. State 2, state 3, and state 4 respiration represent different stages of mitochondrial respiration with varying ATP production. ATPase and electron flow play crucial roles in ATP production within the respiratory chain. Proton leak and proton back-diffusion impact the efficiency of mitochondrial respiration. The terminal electron acceptor is the final molecule in the electron transport chain that accepts electrons. Well-coupled mitochondria are characterized by efficient ATP production and minimal proton leak. Mouse liver mitochondria and mitochondria isolated from cell lines and tissues are different sources used in experiments. Ultrastructure and transmission electron micrographs are techniques used to visualize the internal structure of mitochondria. ADP stimulation and low yield of isolated mitochondria affect the efficiency of mitochondrial isolation. Pellet loss after centrifugation and low quality of isolated mitochondria are challenges in obtaining high-quality mitochondrial preparations. Table 1 and Table 2 contain experimental data related to mitochondrial function and oxygen consumption. Quality control ensures the reliability and functionality of isolated mitochondria in energization processes. Mitochondrial function involves the analysis of cellular respiration and oxygen consumption in mitochondrial preparations. The respiratory chain investigates electron flow and terminal electron acceptors in mitochondrial respiration. State 2, 3, and 4 respiration characterize different stages of mitochondrial respiration with varying ATP production. Substrates and inhibitors are used to study the effects on mitochondrial activity. Mitochondrial isolation poses challenges such as low yield, low quality, and pellet loss. FCCP and added ADP impact mitochondrial oxygen consumption. Transmission electron micrographs visualize mitochondrial structure. summary:
1. The loose part of the pellet contains damaged mitochondria and can be discarded without affecting the overall quality of the preparation.
2. Lipid contamination can occur from the foamy material near the top of the tube. Avoid contact with mitochondria and remove the lipids by wiping the inside of the tube.
3. Unstable oxygen consumption baseline may be due to bacterial or yeast contamination in the buffer. Verify if the buffers are contaminated by repeating the recording with bidistilled water.
4. Inadequate calibration of the instrument may cause unstable baseline. Re-calibrate the instrument.
5. Tears in the polyethylene membrane of the electrode can affect the oxygen concentration recording. Check the response of the oxygraph by transiently stopping stirring and replace the membrane if necessary.
6. If the mitochondrial preparation is not consuming oxygen, it may be due to overestimation of protein concentration. Double the mitochondrial concentration in the chamber.
7. Mechanical and osmotic damage to mitochondria during isolation can be reduced by substituting 0.2 M sucrose with 0.3 M mannitol in the isolation buffer.
8. Contamination by other intracellular membranes can be minimized by washing the mitochondrial pellet with isolation buffer.
9. ADP-stimulated respiration rate may be too low due to the omission of Pi from the buffer. Add Pi to the buffer and check the respiration.
10. High percentage of mitochondria with ruptured outer membranes can be tested by adding exogenous cytochrome c and checking the respiration. If respiration starts, the outer membrane is leaky.
11. Uncoupling by Ca2+ overload can cause unusually high basal respiration. Follow the indicated critical steps to avoid contamination and wash glassware with isolation buffer supplemented with EGTA.
12. Uncoupling by fatty acids can also cause unusually high basal respiration. Include 0.1% fatty acid-free albumin in the experimental buffer and increase FCCP concentration.
13. If the uncoupled respiration rate is lower than the ADP-stimulated respiration, it may be due to using too much FCCP. Titrate down the concentration of FCCP used.
14. The rate of oxygen consumption can increase due to highly pure and intact mitochondria. Check the morphology and purity of the isolated organelles using conventional electron microscopy.
15. The authors acknowledge the support from various organizations and declare no competing financial interests.

summary:
Induction of apoptotic program in cell-free extracts requires dATP and cytochrome c. (Wang, X et al, 1996)

Prevention of apoptosis is achieved by Bcl-2, which blocks the release of cytochrome c from mitochondria. (Yang, J et al, 1997)

Proapoptotic BCL-2 family members induce cytochrome c release through mechanisms that involve mitochondrial fission. (Scorrano, L & Korsmeyer, S.J, 2003)

Dendritic mitochondria play a crucial role in the morphogenesis and plasticity of spines and synapses. (Li, Z et al, 2004)

Mitochondrial genetics have an impact on male infertility. (St John, J.C et al, 2005)

Mitochondrial rhomboid parl regulates cytochrome c release during apoptosis via opa1-dependent cristae remodeling. (Cipolat, S et al, 2006)

Regulation of the permeability transition pore in skeletal muscle mitochondria is modulated by electron flow through the respiratory chain complex I. (Fontaine, E et al, 1998)

OPA1 controls apoptotic cristae remodeling independently from mitochondrial fusion. (Frezza, C et al, 2006)

Mitochondrial fusion intermediates have been observed in vitro. (Meeusen, S et al, 2004)

Methods for isolating brain and heart mitochondria have been established. (Stahl, W.L et al, 1963; Cannon, B & Lindberg, O, 1979; Mela, L & Seitz, S, 1979)

Different sugars have varying effects on rat liver mitochondria. (Siess, E.A, 1983)

The release of cytochrome c from mitochondria induced by Bax is independent of the permeability transition pore but highly dependent on Mg2+ ions. (Eskes, R et al, 1998)

Bioenergetics is a comprehensive study of energy conversion in biological systems. (Nicholls, D.G & Ferguson, S.J, 2002)

topics.justification: The first three summaries discuss the induction and prevention of apoptosis, the role of cytochrome c, and the involvement of Bcl-2 family members.

topics.topic: Role of mitochondria in apoptosis
topics.justification: The summaries mention the release of cytochrome c from mitochondria and the role of mitochondrial fission and cristae remodeling in apoptosis.

topics.topic: Mitochondrial function and its impact on cellular processes
topics.justification: The summaries highlight the role of mitochondria in dendritic morphogenesis, male infertility, energy conversion, and the effects of different sugars on mitochondrial function.

topics.topic: Methods for isolating and studying mitochondria
topics.justification: The summaries mention the establishment of methods for isolating brain and heart mitochondria and the observation of mitochondrial fusion intermediates in vitro.

hypothetical_questions.question: What are the key factors required for the induction of apoptotic program in cell-free extracts?
How does Bcl-2 prevent apoptosis and what is its role in blocking the release of cytochrome c from mitochondria?
Imagine a scenario where a proapoptotic BCL-2 family member induces cytochrome c release through mitochondrial fission. What could be the potential consequences of this mechanism?
How does the role of dendritic mitochondria in the morphogenesis and plasticity of spines and synapses relate to the regulation of cytochrome c release during apoptosis?
Can you think of any other examples where mitochondrial genetics have been found to impact specific physiological processes or conditions?
